# Deeprecursion

> Research notes by Markus Fix. Rough consensus and running code.

## Posts

- [First Light: Loom's Self-Modification Pipeline in 2,214 Lines](https://lispmeister.github.io/deeprecursion/posts/2026-03-16-loom-mvp.md): The MVP field report: 2,214 lines of ClojureScript, 90 tests, 17 generations run. From early failures through five critical bug fixes to a 5/5 stable pipeline. Architecture review findings, generation-by-generation history, the sequence diagram, and what's needed to close the recursive loop.
- [The program.md Protocol: Steering Self-Improvement with a Contract](https://lispmeister.github.io/deeprecursion/posts/2026-03-13-program-md.md): How to steer a self-improving agent with a collaboratively written contract. Refined execution model: user and Prime draft program.md, autonomous Lab executes it, Prime verifies independently, git tracks every generation. State continuity across generations identified as the hard problem.
- [The Prime and the Lab: Recursive Self-Improvement for Coding Agents](https://lispmeister.github.io/deeprecursion/posts/2026-03-12-recursive-self-improvement.md): A ClojureScript agent running in an Apple container, modifying itself, and testing modifications in a second container before promoting them. Architecture spec for recursive self-improvement using homoiconic code, VM-level isolation, and a keep/revert loop.
- [Loom in Lean: Bootstrapping a Verified Self-Improving Agent](https://lispmeister.github.io/deeprecursion/posts/2026-03-11-loom-in-lean.md): Why we moved past Elixir to Lean. The type checker as an incorruptible judge, memory as the beachhead, practical limitations, and an honest assessment of what kills this. **Update:** Lean+QMD memory approach abandoned in favor of ByteRover.
- [The Autoresearch Pattern: A Blueprint for Self-Improving Agents](https://lispmeister.github.io/deeprecursion/posts/2026-03-11-self-improving-agents.md): Karpathy's autoresearch reveals the structure that makes self-improvement work: three separations (judge, subject, process), a keep/revert loop, and well-chosen fixed points.
- [Loom: The Case for Building on Opal](https://lispmeister.github.io/deeprecursion/posts/2026-03-05-loom.md): An existing Elixir port of the Pi coding agent already solves the hardest architectural problem. One architect, frontier models, five weeks.
- [Porting OpenClaw's Core to Elixir](https://lispmeister.github.io/deeprecursion/posts/2026-03-05-porting-openclaw-to-elixir.md): A practical assessment of porting the core gateway and agent loop to Elixir, keeping the TypeScript channels, and bridging them.
- [Runtime Self-Modification: Beyond TypeScript](https://lispmeister.github.io/deeprecursion/posts/2026-03-05-runtime-self-modification.md): Why building a self-modifying agent in TypeScript is writing letters to your future self. Common Lisp offers depth, Elixir/BEAM offers safety.
- [Making OpenClaw Self-Aware: How Can an AI Agent Patch Its Own Code?](https://lispmeister.github.io/deeprecursion/posts/2026-03-05-openclaw-self-modification.md): Five approaches to giving an AI agent a coherent mental model of its own codebase.
- [What if the LLM Thought in S-Expressions?](https://lispmeister.github.io/deeprecursion/posts/2026-02-13-sexp-native-editing.md): Designing an AST-native code editing protocol for LLMs using Lisp's homoiconicity.
- [Lunar Habitat Life Support Systems](https://lispmeister.github.io/deeprecursion/posts/2026-02-13-lunar-life-support-systems.md): Companies, technologies, and supply chains for atmospheric control and CO₂ scrubbing in next-generation lunar stations.

## Optional

- [Full content of all posts](https://lispmeister.github.io/deeprecursion/llms-full.txt)
